problem: Design a system to reduce traffic congestion in a tier-2 Indian city like Indore or Surat.
reference_answer: A strong solution treats congestion as a multi-cause problem: inefficient signal timing, poor last-mile connectivity, over-reliance on private vehicles, and lack of enforcement. High-quality answers propose: adaptive traffic signal control (ML-based, using existing CCTV feeds), a dedicated BRT or mini-bus corridor on the 2-3 highest-traffic arteries, first/last-mile integration (auto-rickshaw feeder routes + cycle lanes), parking pricing to disincentivise car use in city centres, and a real-time congestion data dashboard for residents. Should include a phased implementation plan, estimate of cost, and acknowledge political feasibility constraints.

rubric.feasibility: Proposed solutions work within typical tier-2 city municipal budget (~₹200-500 Cr), use existing infrastructure where possible, and don't require national-level policy changes.
rubric.completeness: Addresses demand reduction (mode shift), supply improvement (signal optimisation, public transit), enforcement, and last-mile connectivity. Has a phased timeline.
rubric.creativity: Goes beyond obvious 'build flyovers' to propose demand management, smart signals, micro-mobility integration, or mobility-as-a-service platforms.
rubric.reasoning_depth: Explains the root causes of congestion (not just symptoms), justifies mode of intervention using traffic engineering or urban planning reasoning, and estimates impact.

problem: Design a functional solid waste management system for an Indian city of 500,000 people that currently has informal waste pickers and no segregation.
reference_answer: Key challenges: no source segregation, informal waste picker livelihoods at risk, inadequate collection, and no processing infrastructure. Strong solutions formalise the existing system without disrupting livelihoods: waste picker cooperatives with ID cards and formal contracts, door-to-door dry/wet segregation collection, decentralised composting at ward level, material recovery facilities partnered with recyclers, and a small user fee for sustainability. Should acknowledge the political sensitivity of formalisation and propose a transition plan.

rubric.feasibility: Works within ULB budget constraints, uses existing informal workforce (formalised), doesn't require large capital-intensive processing plants immediately.
rubric.completeness: Covers collection, segregation at source, processing (wet=compost, dry=recycle), and livelihood protection for informal workers.
rubric.creativity: Proposes waste picker cooperatives, decentralised composting, app-based collection scheduling, or revenue-generating MRFs.
rubric.reasoning_depth: Explains why centralised landfill-based models fail and why the proposed system is more sustainable. Quantifies waste volumes and processing capacity needed.

problem: Design a skilling and employment program for 10,000 unemployed youth (aged 18–30) in a post-industrial district where the main factory has shut down.
reference_answer: Effective programs address multiple failure modes of existing skill programs: irrelevant courses, no job guarantee, geographic barriers, and low completion rates. Strong solutions: skill demand mapping with local/regional employers first, modular short-term courses (3–6 months) tied to real job vacancies, stipend during training (retention), placement cell with employer partnerships, and entrepreneurship track for those not placed. Should address the specific context: post-industrial town likely has metalworking, logistics, or textile skills that can be pivoted.

rubric.feasibility: Financially viable with NSDC/state skilling scheme funding. Doesn't require building new infrastructure from scratch.
rubric.completeness: Covers needs assessment, course design, delivery, stipend/retention, placement, and post-placement tracking.
rubric.creativity: Proposes demand-led skilling (employers define curriculum), RPL (recognition of prior learning), or cluster-based industrial park model.
rubric.reasoning_depth: Explains why standard government skill programs fail (supply-push vs demand-pull) and how the proposed design avoids those failure modes.

problem: How would you build a digital information and market-linkage platform for 50,000 small-scale farmers in a drought-prone district?
reference_answer: Small farmers need four things: timely crop advisory (what to plant, when), weather alerts, fair price discovery, and access to buyers without middleman exploitation. A strong solution integrates IVR/WhatsApp-based crop advisory (works on basic phones), SMS weather alerts, a digital mandi board showing real-time prices, and direct farmer-to-buyer linkage (e-NAM integration or FPO aggregation model). Must address the last-mile constraint: low smartphone penetration, low digital literacy, and connectivity gaps in rural areas.

rubric.feasibility: Works on feature phones, uses existing telecom infrastructure, doesn't require new hardware or high investment per farmer.
rubric.completeness: Covers information access, price discovery, market linkage, and trust-building with farmers.
rubric.creativity: Proposes FPO-based aggregation, voice-first interface, or satellite-based connectivity for remote areas.
rubric.reasoning_depth: Explains the specific information asymmetry problems small farmers face and how each component addresses them. Acknowledges digital divide as a core constraint.

problem: Design a district-level program to reduce the maternal mortality rate (MMR) in a high-MMR district with poor healthcare infrastructure.
reference_answer: High MMR is caused by: delays in recognising danger signs (Delay 1), reaching a facility (Delay 2), and receiving quality care (Delay 3). Strong solutions address all three: ASHA worker training for danger sign recognition + emergency contact protocols, ambulance availability at block level with 24/7 dispatch, first-referral unit upgrade for basic obstetric emergency care, birth waiting homes near CHCs for high-risk pregnancies, and community-level antenatal care tracking through mobile apps. Should cite the Three Delays Model and target specific bottlenecks in the district's data.

rubric.feasibility: Uses existing ASHA/ANM workforce, NHM funding, and existing healthcare infrastructure with targeted upgrades.
rubric.completeness: Addresses all three delays, covers prevention (ANC), detection (danger signs), and emergency response.
rubric.creativity: Proposes birth waiting homes, community obstetric mapping, or digital partograph tracking.
rubric.reasoning_depth: Uses the Three Delays Model or equivalent framework to structure the solution. Explains which delay is the primary bottleneck in this district type.

problem: Design a water scarcity management system for a district that faces severe groundwater depletion and 4-month seasonal water scarcity every year.
reference_answer: Effective water management combines supply augmentation (rainwater harvesting, check dams, watershed development) with demand reduction (micro-irrigation, crop change incentives, industrial water audits) and governance (gram sabha-level water budgets, groundwater regulation). Strong answers acknowledge the tragedy of the commons problem, propose community-managed water institutions, and include a monitoring system (groundwater level sensors + public dashboard). Should address the agricultural sector as the primary water consumer (70%+).

rubric.feasibility: Uses MGNREGA funds for watershed work, state irrigation schemes, and existing panchayat institutions.
rubric.completeness: Covers demand reduction, supply augmentation, governance/regulation, and monitoring.
rubric.creativity: Proposes water budgeting at gram panchayat level, crop diversification incentives, or market-based water rights.
rubric.reasoning_depth: Explains the groundwater commons problem and why individual solutions fail without collective governance. Quantifies water balance (inflow vs. extraction).

problem: Design a dropout prevention system for a district with 30% middle school dropout rates, primarily affecting girls and first-generation learners.
reference_answer: Dropout is caused by economic pressure (child labour), distance, early marriage, lack of toilets (for girls), and perceived low ROI of education. Strong solutions are multi-pronged: conditional cash transfer for continued enrolment (especially girls), girls' dormitories for long-distance commuters, separate functional toilets, community-based tracking of at-risk students by ASHA/MNREGA workers, teacher engagement training, and bridge programs for re-entry. Should differentiate between factors affecting girls vs boys, and first-gen vs other learners.

rubric.feasibility: Uses Samagra Shiksha, NMSA funds, and existing anganwadi/ASHA infrastructure.
rubric.completeness: Addresses economic, infrastructure, social, and academic factors. Has re-entry pathway for dropouts.
rubric.creativity: Proposes community tracking dashboards, AI-based early warning (attendance + grades), or peer-to-peer mentoring.
rubric.reasoning_depth: Disaggregates dropout causes by gender and SES, explains why universal solutions fail, and targets specific barriers for each group.

problem: How would you reduce air pollution in a heavily industrialised city with high PM2.5 levels and a large brick kiln and diesel vehicle population?
reference_answer: PM2.5 sources in this context: brick kilns (coal combustion), diesel vehicles (transport), and road dust. Strong solutions target each source: zig-zag kiln technology retrofit (60% emission reduction, govt subsidised), vehicle age-based emission testing and phase-out of pre-BS4 vehicles, dust suppression on unpaved roads, green buffer zones around industrial areas, and a real-time air quality monitoring network with public alerts. Should propose a source apportionment study first to prioritise the biggest contributors, and include an enforcement mechanism.

rubric.feasibility: Uses NCAP funding, existing PUC system, and CPCB guidelines. Brick kiln retrofit is cost-effective with government subsidy.
rubric.completeness: Addresses industrial, transport, and fugitive dust sources. Includes monitoring and enforcement.
rubric.creativity: Proposes source apportionment as first step, community air quality monitors, or green commute incentives.
rubric.reasoning_depth: Explains which source contributes most to PM2.5 in this context and why, justifies prioritisation of interventions based on impact-to-cost ratio.

problem: Design a financial inclusion program to bring 100,000 unbanked rural households into the formal financial system within 2 years.
reference_answer: Barriers to banking: distance (nearest bank 15 km), documentation (no ID/address proof), distrust, and low financial literacy. Strong solutions use the JAM trinity model (Jan Dhan + Aadhaar + Mobile): open zero-balance accounts via Aadhaar-based KYC at BC agents (banking correspondents), activate UPI/USSD payments for feature phones, introduce micro-insurance and micro-savings products, and run financial literacy camps through SHG networks. Should acknowledge that account opening is not financial inclusion — active usage is — and propose usage incentives.

rubric.feasibility: Uses existing PMJDY, BC agent network, and Aadhaar infrastructure. No new tech required.
rubric.completeness: Covers account opening, activation, usage, financial literacy, and product access (credit, insurance, savings).
rubric.creativity: Proposes SHG-led financial literacy, game-based financial education, or WhatsApp-based customer service.
rubric.reasoning_depth: Distinguishes between account ownership and financial inclusion (active use), explains barrier stack, and proposes solutions that target the deepest barriers.

problem: How would you design an affordable housing solution for 50,000 migrant workers living in slums near an industrial zone?
reference_answer: Migrant worker housing fails because: private rental is exploitative and unsafe, government housing ignores proximity-to-work need, and mobility (seasonal migration) makes long-term housing impractical. Strong solutions propose: employer-linked dormitory model (factory as developer under CSR/ESG pressure), government night shelter upgrades with basic amenities, rent voucher scheme for the bottom income segment, community kitchens to reduce living costs, and portability of ration/welfare benefits so migrant workers aren't locked to a home state. Should address both permanent and seasonal migrants differently.

rubric.feasibility: Uses CSR mandate, PMAY subsidy, and local ULB land assets. Doesn't require massive greenfield construction.
rubric.completeness: Covers housing access, affordability, amenities, and welfare portability. Differentiates seasonal vs permanent migrants.
rubric.creativity: Proposes dormitory-as-infrastructure-bond (employer guaranteed), portable welfare ID, or modular housing units.
rubric.reasoning_depth: Explains why existing housing programs miss migrant workers (eligibility criteria, mobility constraints) and how the proposed solution addresses those specific gaps.

problem: Design a functional, corruption-resistant public distribution system (PDS) for a district with 200,000 BPL beneficiaries.
reference_answer: PDS corruption happens at diversion (grain diverted to open market), ghost beneficiaries (fake cards), and exclusion (real beneficiaries not getting grain). Strong solutions: Aadhaar-linked biometric authentication at fair price shops (eliminates ghosts and fake cards), end-to-end computerised supply chain tracking (from FCI godown to FPS), GPS tracking on PDS trucks, social audit by gram sabhas, and a grievance redressal hotline with 24-hour SLA. Should also address exclusion error (genuine poor excluded due to biometric failure) with a manual override process.

rubric.feasibility: Uses existing ONORC, Aadhaar ePoS, and FPS infrastructure. No major new systems required.
rubric.completeness: Covers ghost elimination, supply chain integrity, exclusion error handling, and grievance redressal.
rubric.creativity: Proposes social audit model, community monitoring dashboard, or dynamic beneficiary list (exit poor who cross income threshold).
rubric.reasoning_depth: Explains the specific leakage mechanisms in PDS, estimates leakage percentage, and shows how each intervention targets a specific mechanism.

problem: Design a functional e-governance portal for a district administration that currently handles 50,000 citizen service requests per year through manual, in-person processes.
reference_answer: Successful e-governance needs three things: citizen-facing simplicity, backend integration, and trust. Strong solutions propose: single-window digital portal (DigiLocker-linked for documents, Aadhaar for identity), 5 most common services digitised first (income certificate, caste certificate, domicile, birth certificate, land records), CSC-based assisted access for digitally excluded citizens, internal workflow management system (track applications, escalation timers), and public dashboard showing application status. Must address offline access, official resistance (change management), and authentication edge cases.

rubric.feasibility: Uses NIC infrastructure, DigiLocker, Aadhaar, and CSC network. Doesn't require building from scratch.
rubric.completeness: Covers citizen interface, backend integration, offline access, change management, and monitoring.
rubric.creativity: Proposes status SMS notifications, automated document verification, or proactive service delivery (push vs pull).
rubric.reasoning_depth: Explains why e-governance portals fail (official resistance, poor UX, no backend integration) and how each design decision addresses a failure mode.

problem: Design a flood early warning and response system for a coastal district with a history of cyclone-related flooding.
reference_answer: Effective early warning requires: accurate forecasting (IMD integration), community alert dissemination (multi-channel: siren, SMS, WhatsApp, TV), pre-positioned evacuation resources, trained local responders at village level, and a clear chain of command. Strong solutions also address the last mile: how does the warning reach a fisherman at sea or an elderly person with no smartphone? Proposals should include community emergency response teams (CERTs), colour-coded alert levels (easy to understand), designated evacuation routes, and post-flood relief pre-positioning. Should distinguish cyclone warning from flood response.

rubric.feasibility: Uses IMD data feeds, NDMA protocols, and existing panchayat infrastructure. Community-trained responders are low-cost.
rubric.completeness: Covers forecasting, alert dissemination, evacuation, response, and relief. Has both early warning and response phases.
rubric.creativity: Proposes fishermen-specific radio alerts, community siren networks, or drone-based flood mapping during disaster.
rubric.reasoning_depth: Explains the specific failure modes of flood response (last-mile alert gap, evacuation bottlenecks) and how each element addresses them.

problem: Design a solar energy adoption program for 20,000 rural households that currently have unreliable grid power (8 hours/day).
reference_answer: Poor grid reliability + falling solar costs makes solar attractive but adoption barriers remain: upfront cost, financing access, technical maintenance, and energy literacy. Strong solutions: PM-KUSUM or similar subsidy scheme for rooftop solar, ESCO-based (pay-as-you-save) financing model to eliminate upfront cost, local technician training (30 in the district), community solar microgrids for clusters of houses too dispersed for individual systems, and productive use appliances (solar pumps, solar mills) for economic ROI. Should distinguish grid-connected (net metering) from off-grid based on location.

rubric.feasibility: Uses PM-KUSUM, RESCO model, and MNRE schemes. No exotic technology required.
rubric.completeness: Covers financing, installation, maintenance, energy literacy, and productive use.
rubric.creativity: Proposes community solar microgrids, productive use assets, or solar entrepreneurs as local technicians.
rubric.reasoning_depth: Explains the cost-benefit for a household with 8-hour grid supply, justifies the ESCO model over subsidy-heavy approach.

problem: Design a district-level program to control the rising incidence of Type 2 diabetes and hypertension among adults aged 35–60.
reference_answer: NCD burden is rising due to: dietary changes (refined carbs, processed food), sedentary lifestyles, and lack of screening. Strong solutions combine prevention and management: population-level screening camps at PHC/CHC level (using CBAC questionnaire), community health worker-managed chronic disease registries, standard treatment protocols for tier 2 health workers, medicine supply chain for chronic disease drugs at SC/PHC level, and preventive intervention (dietary counselling, yoga/exercise clubs at mohalla level). Should acknowledge that most patients will be caught late (Stage 2+) and that management is the near-term priority.

rubric.feasibility: Uses NHM NPCDCS scheme, existing PHC/CHC infrastructure, and ASHA/ANM workforce.
rubric.completeness: Covers screening, diagnosis, treatment protocol, medication access, and long-term follow-up.
rubric.creativity: Proposes mohalla health clubs, telehealth specialist consultation for complicated cases, or dynamic patient risk stratification.
rubric.reasoning_depth: Explains the NCD epidemiological transition, distinguishes between prevention and management priorities, and explains the bottleneck in the existing system.

problem: How would you design an accessible city for people with disabilities in an existing Indian city without a large infrastructure overhaul budget?
reference_answer: Accessibility is often treated as a renovation problem but the bigger barriers are design standards, enforcement, and social inclusion. Strong solutions: mandatory accessibility audit of top 50 high-use public buildings and retrofitting (ramps, tactile paths, accessible toilets) within 2 years, accessible bus stops (lowered kerbs, audio announcements) on 5 main routes, accessible pedestrian crossings (audible signals, designated island), disability-inclusive urban planning guidelines for all new construction, and a citizen-led accessibility reporting app. Should engage DPO (disabled persons organisation) as co-designers.

rubric.feasibility: Focuses on low-cost retrofits (tactile paving, ramps, signage) and enforcement of existing RPWD Act 2016 standards.
rubric.completeness: Covers built environment, transport, information access, and enforcement/monitoring.
rubric.creativity: Proposes DPO co-design, accessibility hackathons, or disability inclusion as a city brand.
rubric.reasoning_depth: Explains the CRPD/RPWD Act framework, distinguishes physical from cognitive and sensory disabilities, and prioritises interventions by impact.

problem: Design a mental health outreach program for rural areas in a district with no psychiatrist within 100 km.
reference_answer: Rural mental health failures: no specialist, high stigma, no help-seeking, no early detection. Solutions use the task-shifting model: train ASHAs and ANMs to identify common mental health conditions (depression, anxiety, psychosis) using validated screening tools (PHQ-9, ASSIST), establish tele-psychiatry links to district hospital (monthly consultation slots), form self-help groups for common mental disorders at village level, integrate mental health screening into existing ASHA home visit protocols, and run community-level de-stigmatisation campaigns through folk media. Should acknowledge the DMHP (District Mental Health Programme) and explain why it's underperforming.

rubric.feasibility: Uses DMHP funding, existing ASHA workforce, and free tele-psychiatry platforms (e-Sanjeevani).
rubric.completeness: Covers early detection, treatment (task-shifted), specialist access (tele), stigma reduction, and community support.
rubric.creativity: Proposes folk media de-stigmatisation, village-level SHG for mental health, or integrating mental health into ASHA incentive structure.
rubric.reasoning_depth: Explains the task-shifting model, explains why DMHP fails currently, and justifies each component with evidence from similar programs (SNEHA, VISHRAM).

problem: How would you make street food safer in a major Indian city with 50,000 street vendors without banning or displacing vendors?
reference_answer: Street food safety requires: vendor registration and health card system (regular medical checkup), food hygiene training (WHO 5 keys to safer food), clean water access at vending points (mobile water tanker routes + tap connections), colour-coded grading certificates displayed at stalls, and consumer awareness. Strong solutions avoid criminalising vendors and instead propose an enabling regulatory framework — licensed vending zones (SVUSV Act 2014 compliant), cluster-based hand-washing stations, and civic society involvement in enforcement. Should distinguish between high-risk foods (cut fruits, chutneys) and lower-risk foods.

rubric.feasibility: Uses FSSAI's Eat Right India framework, SVUSV Act, and municipal health department. No major new investment.
rubric.completeness: Covers vendor training, water access, grading, consumer awareness, and regulatory framework.
rubric.creativity: Proposes star grading visible to consumers, vendor WhatsApp training modules, or food safety champions among vendors.
rubric.reasoning_depth: Explains the specific food safety risks in street food context (water source, temperature, cross-contamination), identifies highest-risk categories, and designs proportionate responses.

problem: Design a sports and physical activity program for school children in a city where 80% of schools have no playground.
reference_answer: No playground is a real constraint — good solutions don't just say 'build playgrounds'. Alternatives: flexible use of school corridors/rooftops for physical activity, local public parks scheduled for school use in morning hours, partnership with nearby private sports clubs for after-school access, curriculum-integrated movement breaks (5-minute activity every 45 minutes), sports ambassadors program (older students lead younger ones), and interschool micro-tournament model (uses 1 shared municipal ground). Should mention the Khelo India program as a potential funding source.

rubric.feasibility: Works without playground construction — uses creative space repurposing and public infrastructure.
rubric.completeness: Covers school-hours activity, after-school sport, teacher capacity, competition, and health tracking.
rubric.creativity: Proposes rooftop activity zones, corridor gymnastics, shared public park scheduling, or movement breaks in curriculum.
rubric.reasoning_depth: Explains the physical and cognitive benefits of activity for school children, explains why traditional playground-centric thinking fails in dense urban India.

problem: How would you build a functional startup ecosystem in a tier-3 Indian city with a strong engineering college but no investors, mentors, or startup culture?
reference_answer: Tier-3 startup ecosystems fail because: no deal flow for investors (chicken-and-egg), no mentors with startup experience, and no culture of risk-taking. Strong solutions start with what exists: the engineering college as the anchor institution (ideation hub, technical talent), connect to nearest tier-1 ecosystem remotely (Bangalore/Hyderabad mentors via virtual office hours), create a small campus fund (alumni seed fund of ₹50L), run hackathons and prototype challenges to generate deal flow, and work with district collector to enable regulatory ease (DPIIT recognition assistance). Should propose a 3-year evolution model.

rubric.feasibility: Uses the college as anchor, remote mentors, and small alumni seed capital. Doesn't require VC-level funding immediately.
rubric.completeness: Covers talent, mentorship, funding, culture, and regulatory support. Has a phased evolution plan.
rubric.creativity: Proposes reverse mentorship (college students mentor local businesses), remote-first investor connections, or district-government partnership.
rubric.reasoning_depth: Explains the chicken-and-egg problem in ecosystem building, proposes sequenced interventions to break the cycle, and sets realistic milestones.
